title: Reading and Use of English – Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text.

content.title: The Razor’s Edge: The Mental Game of Elite Athletics
content.subtitle: Why psychological resilience often outweighs physical prowess in the final moments of competition.
content.text: In the upper echelons of professional sport, the physical capabilities of competitors are often nearly identical. When eight sprinters line up for an Olympic final, they all possess world-class lung capacity, explosive fast-twitch fibers, and meticulously managed diets. [31] What separates the gold medalist from the rest is rarely a physical attribute, but rather the ability to maintain cognitive composure under the suffocating weight of expectation. [/31] This 'mental toughness' is not an innate trait but a psychological skill that can be developed through rigorous training. || Central to this training is the concept of 'arousal regulation.' Every athlete has an optimal level of physiological excitement; too little and they are sluggish, too much and they suffer from 'choking.' [32] The phenomenon of choking occurs when the conscious mind tries to take control of a motor skill that has become automatic through years of practice. [/32] By over-analyzing their technique in a moment of high pressure, the athlete disrupts the fluid, subconscious execution of the movement, leading to a catastrophic drop in performance. || To combat this, sport psychologists use 'visual-motor behavior rehearsal.' This involves more than just picturing a win; it requires the athlete to engage all their senses to create a vivid mental simulation of the event. [33] By repeatedly 'experiencing' the pressure of the stadium and the physical sensations of the race in their mind, the athlete desensitizes themselves to the stress of the actual moment. [/33] When they finally step onto the track, the brain perceives the environment as familiar rather than threatening, allowing them to remain in a 'flow state.' || [34] The author’s description of 'perceived competence' suggests it is the most critical factor in an athlete’s persistence. [/34] An athlete who believes they have the tools to succeed will view a setback as a temporary hurdle, whereas one with low perceived competence will see the same event as a confirmation of their inadequacy. This self-fulfilling prophecy determines how much effort an athlete will exert when they are exhausted. It is the difference between a player who fights for a lost ball in the final minute and one who mentally surrenders before the whistle blows. || [35] Critics of the current focus on 'grit' argue that it can lead to toxic outcomes, such as athletes ignoring serious injuries to prove their toughness. [/35] This is a valid concern; resilience should not be confused with recklessness. A truly resilient athlete knows when to push through pain and when to listen to their body to ensure longevity. The goal of sport psychology is to build a sustainable mindset, not one that burns out in the pursuit of a single victory. || Looking ahead, the next revolution in training will likely involve 'neurofeedback,' where athletes can see their brainwaves in real-time. [36] By learning to modulate their neural activity, competitors may soon be able to 'dial in' the exact mental state required for peak performance at will. [/36] As the margins of physical improvement continue to shrink, the final frontier of sport remains the mastery of the human mind.

questions.question: In the first paragraph, the author suggests that the deciding factor in elite sports is...
options: A) an athlete's unique genetic physical advantages.
B) the ability to handle psychological pressure.
C) a superior nutritional and dietary regimen.
D) an innate talent that cannot be taught.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [31], emphasizing 'cognitive composure under the suffocating weight of expectation' over physical attributes.

questions.question: According to the second paragraph, 'choking' is the result of...
options: A) a lack of sufficient physical training.
B) the conscious mind interfering with automatic movements.
C) a decrease in physiological arousal and excitement.
D) the brain's inability to analyze technique during a race.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [32], which explains choking as the 'conscious mind' trying to take control of an 'automatic' skill.

questions.question: What is the primary goal of 'visual-motor behavior rehearsal'?
options: A) To help athletes ignore the physical sensations of their sport.
B) To make the competitive environment feel less intimidating.
C) To increase the level of stress felt during the actual event.
D) To replace physical practice with mental simulations.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [33], stating that mental rehearsal 'desensitizes' the athlete, making the environment feel 'familiar rather than threatening.'

questions.question: In the fourth paragraph, 'perceived competence' is linked to...
options: A) the speed at which an athlete can recover from an injury.
B) an athlete's willingness to keep trying during difficult times.
C) the social status an athlete achieves after winning.
D) a natural talent for avoiding all setbacks.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [34], which notes it is the factor in 'persistence' and how much effort is exerted when 'exhausted.'

questions.question: The author mentions 'grit' to illustrate that...
options: A) psychological resilience is always a positive attribute.
B) athletes should be encouraged to play through any pain.
C) there is a risk of taking mental toughness to a dangerous extreme.
D) sport psychology consultants prioritize single victories over longevity.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [35], where critics warn that focusing on grit can lead to 'ignoring serious injuries.'

questions.question: In the final paragraph, 'neurofeedback' is presented as a way for athletes to...
options: A) monitor their physical diet more effectively.
B) gain an unfair advantage over those without the technology.
C) consciously control their mental state for the best results.
D) replace the need for sport psychologists entirely.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [36], stating athletes can 'modulate their neural activity' and 'dial in' the required mental state.
